speaker: interviewer
text: --the 30th, 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler interviewing Ludwig Weiler. This is tape nine. So we've-- you're liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated. I was in hospital. When I came out of hospital, eventually that group was put together. We travelled on all means of transport. We didn't want to come under Russian control, because we didn't know whether we will have the same freedom of movement as while the Americans have it. And there are a lot of anecdotes as to what happened in the camp after the war. I have personal knowledge of the fact that there were some colored-- black-- American GIs who were very decent. They have taken particular attention that wherever an SS man was, or somebody, they finished them off there and there. There was a certain amount of quietly kept justice being carried out. Unfortunately, to a very small extent, because the American military it was unacceptable to. So these were individual, private actions where certain people-- because they caught some of the guards there. They were not necessarily Germans. They were just SS. Ukrainians, all sorts of people. And unfortunately not very many, but a few were finished off there.

speaker: interviewer
text: There are many anecdotes about Buchenwald when the Americans came in. And one I would like to repeat, which is, I think, typical of the attitude of the people who survived and went through those problems is-- which has been recorded and written about often-- is when an American officer asked a young boy shortly after the liberation of Buchenwald, how old are you? And he answered, I'm older than you. And the reason said, because I don't laugh, and I don't cry. And this is relatively true.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, psychologically there were two groups of people-- the ones who got mentally and emotionally broken. Some mentally and emotionally, and some only emotionally. Then there were some who were physically broken. I mean, even I looked like a stick. And I was on the border or survival. But that you recover. The emotional scars is a different matter. And then, particularly among the young people-- and it depended on the strength of your background, I suppose, and of your nature-- to be a-- have a will to survive and be prepared to fight for it. And that was, as I said earlier, very, very important. And I would say that probably most of those were the ones who originally, after the war, went out via Cyprus and everything to Israel who were the fighters in the First Israeli War of Independence. And those people understood that politics are all very well for Switzerland, for Denmark, for England, where peace is reigning. But when you deal with a gun, you can only handle it with a gun. And all nice words, assurances, don't matter. And I come from that generation. Now, having gone back on the transport, there were some interesting anecdotes. We travelled practically on hourly or half-daily basis. We got off one train, because it stopped, and we had to find another one. And eventually we got to the place called Bamberg, in central Germany. And I remember they took, in the middle of the day, away the engine. They commandeered it away, the American military. So everybody got off. It was a coal train. And there were Yugoslavs and all nationalities. We all got off. There was a field with onions and potatoes, and there was a little creek. We had a marvelous meal for a day. Anyway, eventually, as we progressed downwards in Germany, I remember one night I was-- we got onto an oil-- you know, these big oil tanker carriers, they've got a big metal plate on the side. So I was thin enough, and so on, so I spent the whole night, while the train was traveling, behind that plate. And I suppose, with normal thinking, it was crazy to do it, but one was quite sort of unafraid already of anything. And one thing I forgot to tell you, which I personally can say on the emotional side, after the war, when I got to know-- immediately after the liberation, I read myself the records-- of what happened to my father and my brother, there was one day when I was terribly upset. And I was crying and whatnot. And I said to myself, right, there are two ways we can get over that. Either I jump out-- it was on the third floor in the hospital-- and that's the end of it, or I will soldier on and, regardless of what happened, carry on. And fortunately, I [LAUGHS] liked myself probably too much. I decided on the latter one. But there was a crisis at all times. It-- I don't think there was a single person who didn't have, at a particular time, a crisis. I would like to mention here something which is maybe a very dangerous subject-- the religion and the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unfortunately, the religious-- very religious Hasidic Jewry of Europe-- got decimated and lost. I mean, the Polish Jewry of three and a half million people, hardly 35,000 survived, which most of them survived in Russia and not in the camps. But that was a six-year period different than in Hungary. And I must say that, after the war, nobody had an answer to what has happened. The irreligious ones said, it all happened because you walked around in beards and payos. The religious ones said, it all happened because you tried to fraternize and assimilate and treat yourself like if you are a non-Jew, and they knew you were a Jew. So there was two opposing corners. And the least, I must say, had to say the rabbis, because they also had no answer to it, particularly the ones who went through the camp. And I must say a thing of the-- one of the extreme Hasidic movements today of America, the Satmars, who are many points very extreme. And the Satmar also in Bergen-Belsen. And afterwards, he got to Switzerland, because he was taken out with that Kastmer transport. And I'm not a Satmar. Of course, I'm not [INAUDIBLE] at all with a perfectly legitimate Ashkenazim and not Hasidim. Said that anybody who went through the camps, and the Holocaust generally, and doesn't keep anything, he has got no question to ask him. He can't say anything to him. Now, that is a great statement, I think. And basically I think till this day, whoever touches that subject from a religious point of view is getting himself into hot water and trouble. Because with our normal thinking, it's difficult to fathom and place it in the right context. You can blame it on anybody. The fact is, it happened. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your own religious faith has remained as strong?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, now that is interesting to say. I have a very secularly modern, but a strictly Orthodox upbringing. And so has all my family, all around. And when I came back and I met my uncle, my mother's brothers, who were the same style of religion and remained so, one of the first questions my uncle asked me, are you still religious? Now, you can deduct from-- deduce from that that even in his mind it was obvious that after what happened it could have very easily been that I wasn't. So there wasn't a single person-- and I maintain that very strongly, and I don't care what anybody says-- who went through it and came back with the same belief and conviction, without any doubt or question , as they went there. I don't believe it if anybody tells you. They may have done shiva, they may have even become more religious and more Hasidic, even become rebbes. I don't care. But that-- this event has weakened a certain link-- in some more, in some less-- in the unshakable belief of God and religion. I have no doubt about it. And I always say, when certain things are discussed-- this can't happen or can't be done-- and my answer to them is, everything can happen and everything can be done. It only needs the circumstances to be like that, and it can happen. And that is human nature. I'm sorry I was philosophizing. Anyway, I got back on this transport to Passau, which is the upper end of the Inn. And, stupidly enough, in my condition, I went swimming. I caught, promptly, a pneumonia. And by the time we got to Linz, in Austria, I was in the crisis day of my pneumonia and the group threatened to leave me behind. And I told them not to do it. But-- and they-- we went out. For two days I was in a railway wagon on Linz railway station going through a very high-temperature pneumonia. But not even a doctor. I don't even remember what-- any medicine. Maybe I got some tablets or whatnot. And then we went out, shortly, the day after, two days after, to Innsbruck, which was the Russian-American border in the partition of Austria. And there we were greeted that unless we turn around right away-- don't forget, it was a private group-- and go back to Linz, they're going to arrest us and put us into a Nazi camp. [LAUGHS] We said, thank you very much. So we all nicely got back to Linz. And there we were informed, no way can we get across unless we go into a DP camp from where they will organize it. Now, we had a-- an extremely strong antagonism to be put under control of a camp-- of any control. That was-- that was the worst thing anybody could suggest. But after a day or two consultation amongst ourself-- and we were sitting around at the railway station-- we decided we had no option. We got into that camp, and literally within a few days, they put together a transport-- perfectly normal-- and we were sent south to Graz, which was English zone. And from there the train was turned around and went up to Wiener Neustadt, which is between Vienna and where I come from. And that's where we got off the train. That's where I met the Russian soldiers for the first time in my life. And we thought we'd just get on the next train. We asked which way the train is going, and the next thing [LAUGHS] he pointed the machine gun at us and in some very rude Russian words, which later on I knew to understand, said that unless we come off, he shoots us on the spot. Anyway, we understood the message. We got off. A half an hour later, we got onto another train. And I was back within an hour in our town. And quickly news spread that I arrived. My cousin met me, because I went to the Jewish center, where the Jewish community offices were. So he picked me up from there. He took me home. That is my first cousin, Professor Gershon Weiler. And he was living together with two friends of his in his parents' flat. So they immediately accommodated me. And the food they gave me consisted of a slivovitz and some chocolates and whatnot, which was totally wrong for my condition. But [LAUGHS] but-- I mean, they were very caring. And they looked after me. Anyway, a few days later, my uncle-- or a week later-- came down from Budapest. this is the one-- the doctor who survived-- and looked at me and decided to put me straight into hospital, which I violently objected. But they x-rayed me, and they found that my lungs had still shadows from my pneumonia, and I was in a weak condition and whatnot. And I must go in for a couple of week. So I did. And there I got drip-fed daily. And my veins were like an old person's, and they kept on jumping so sometimes they had to stick the needle into me five, six times till they found-- till they got my veins. And one day, I just about had enough, and I jumped up. And I said, no more today. I said, forget it. I'm not letting you. Being after the war and such shortage of serums, they give it to an elderly patient there, a non-Jew, in the hospital in Sopron. And the guy got instantly a heart attack  on it. And the doctor told me afterwards I was very lucky, because if I would have got that bad serum in my condition, I would have not survived it. So there goes another story how you jump the fence, unbeknown to you. Thereafter, I went down to a town called Papa, where my aunt, who is still alive, my father's younger sister, Margit-- her married name was Kohn-- Weiler, maiden name. She is the one, with her two children, who were taken out of the ghetto and taken to Budapest from that transport. Unfortunately, her husband has not returned, because he was arrested, as I told you earlier. And I spent two-- a couple of months there. I have a cousin who is an architect who I took out to Australia and my aunt in 1956, after the revolution, when I already lived in Australia. And a sister, who still lives in Budapest. And I sort of recuperated there. I-- we used to go with my cousin to the cellars where they threw all the Jewish books and [INAUDIBLE] and everything and chumashim and mishnayot and everything. And we literally worked days on end-- as much energy as one had-- to put them together, because nobody really cared. And sometime you found your own family's books and all that. You know, that's the sort of occupation I had. And then, nevertheless, after that, I went over to Szombathely, which is very close by, to my uncle's-- my mother's brother-- as we were quite close-- very close, always. And, you know, somehow they had no children, and I fitted better into it. And I was the youngest in the whole community there. There was practically a 15-year age gap for the next lot of-- no, it wasn't quite. But there was a gap. Below my age, nobody. Don't forget, as I said earlier, in our town I was the only person who survived under the age of 18. And the same story went on in other towns. I remember in Szombathely there was another boy who was a couple of years older than me, but there was no company. Nobody. Financially, I didn't need any assistance. My parents left a lot of property behind. From my grandfather's businesses and income was-- we had more businesses than we could manage. And literally, for the following eight, nine months, I was recuperating.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't forget the [INAUDIBLE] was also affected. My hair partly fell out, but it grew back afterwards. And they used to say that if somebody was a little bit off in their head, that you had fleck, didn't you? Because the [INAUDIBLE] is an illness which affects some parts of the body, either the legs or the heart or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got, in fact, a bit of a sports heart. And I was told not to drink and not to smoke. Drink, I still don't. I do smoke sometime. It's not for the American audience. Anyway, so I was there for that few months helping in-- generally in the business. I learned. I didn't go back anymore to school. I studied some Jewish subject. But by August, 1946, a brother of my aunt by marriage, whose parents had a very big leather business in a place called Sarvar-- loving family-- I was told that, here are the keys. I'm going to England. That brother of hers, [INAUDIBLE] was-- is his name. He lives in Australia. And you just sell out and close the shop and finish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went over there. I thought it was quite an interesting exercise. It was a business which had a couple of employees. And-- and-- and was the boss, so to speak. Can you imagine? Somebody 16 years and four months?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I literally took over a business which I never knew about anything. I remember the first time I went up to Budapest to purchase, my uncle from Szombathely came with me just to chaperone me. But we managed all right. And from then on, I ran that business single-handedly with the employees. And I used to travel by air from Szombathely to Budapest. Sometimes I went to the other end of the country. I brought the goods by air. I was known as the youngest leather merchant in the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: The wholesalers, who were quite a lot of Jewish people, were very supportive of me, because the-- the skill was to get the goods. Sell? You could sell. There was no problem. There was a shortage of everything. And the profit margin was huge.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, you know, being a 16-, 17-year-old boy, I was doing very well, thank you very much. Because maybe the way I talk, maybe because of my age, I-- I-- they helped me. I didn't look like that I needed financial help. In fact, I had plenty of capital to run the business. But I needed the goodwill of the people to supply me. Anyway, that was my activity over the period. I must tell you-- which is a Holocaust-- sub-Holocaust story-- in 1946 in Romania, was the first really serious Stalinist Communism under [INAUDIBLE], who was the prime minister. And the Jews were trying to leave Romania. One of the few ways they could was to come into Hungary and cross the country and go out through Australia. The borders were relatively open. It wasn't so easy, but it wasn't so difficult, either. But you could not travel through without papers. The Bricha or [INAUDIBLE]-- you know, the Israeli connection-- organized a mass emigration through various sources. Some through Kashau, Slovakia; and through Bratislava into Austria; and some through Hungary, through Szombathely. And Sopron, as well. I lived in Szombathely. And my uncle, [INAUDIBLE] Arya Yahuda was his name. He died five years ago. He was born in 1906, and he went through Auschwitz, Buchenwald-- Auschwitz, Birkenau, ending up in Minsk, in Russia. Because when Russians liberated, they took them the wrong way, back to Minsk, in January, '45. And he was the president. My-- his father, my grandfather, was president for many, many years before the war. And he was in charge of it. We had tremendous properties. And one of the properties had two-- from the main square and the main street entrance. So you could literally back in huge trucks and disappear, because there was high walls and high gates. Nobody would know. And I remember, day after day, only about 10 doors away was the-- this organization's office, local office, who looked after the immigrants or these refugees coming-- Romanians. And constantly, every night, they just came quietly in in pairs, in twos and threes, into our property on one end, and they waited there till it became dark. And we had it organized with Russian military-- Jewish Russian military-- who were paid or not paid or whatever-- that night after night they were taking them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So literally thousands, if not tens of thousands, of people went through that route. I personally used to-- as a 17-, 18-year-old, it was after business hours, no problem. It was always at night, in the darkness-- I helped the people with their belongings, with their luggage. Sometime, older people, to help them up on the trucks. And we gave them food and sometime clothing. You know, we helped them sort of to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this went on till 1948. And suddenly, one night, the Hungarian guards, or Russians, I'm not sure, shot at one of these trucks because they didn't stop at the border crossing. They killed one person, and then it became known that this escape route is going on. And suddenly, a few days before Tisha B'Av, my uncle was arrested. We had good connection with the police. Plenty paid for. After two, three days, he was released. But that was the end of it. Then there was no more. And in 19-- this was in 1948. But in 1947, we-- we often had a lot of Jewish Russian soldiers coming into the business. And sometime they-- we invited them to the house and so on. And they used to exchange views. They spoke Yiddish, we spoke German. You know-- we could understand each other. And I'll never forget, there was a Russian captain, about a 45-year-old man in 1947, who came up to-- into our house. First we met him in the shop, and then we invited him for a meal and whatnot. And he said to us, in Yiddish like that, [YIDDISH]. The [YIDDISH] means 'the Russian thief' has got us. I've got a mother. I've got a father. I can't. But you people should know to get away while you can. We didn't know what he was talking about. There was a coalition government in Hungary. The Communists held some important portfolios, but it was normal life. By the summer of 1948, just at the time when my uncle was arrested, things have changed. The Communists fused with Socialists under obvious pressure. I noticed that one of my trips, as I was coming back from the Budapest, I come at the station where I just walk out, you know. This guy comes to me, identify yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I look at him. I say, what do you want? He said, identify yourself. And he shows me he's from the security police. So at that time they started checking the people who were arriving already on the border towns.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, that was that. I told you my lovely experience of Mr. Cardinal Mindszenty's speech earlier, I think. And in April, after Pesach, 1949, I decided that Hungary and I will part company. I didn't like them. They didn't do us many favors. I certainly wasn't interested to come under a Communist-dominated era where Jews were also considered as unreliable, capitalist, bourgeois, whatever names they fancied to give. And I escaped one Sunday afternoon. I went out-- I paid $100, which was quite a lot of money in those days, to a guy who owned a grape plantation on both sides of the border. You know, vine-- vineyards. And the-- the barbed wires were there already. I could describe it-- two sets of wire by one and a half meter high, cross and [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, but it wasn't mined. I went out with a-- from a comfortable home with everything left behind except what I managed to send ahead-- some silver and Persian carpets and whatnot, so a few belongings, which I got back very few, after the war-- which I sent out. And I paid a lot of money for somebody who was probably not worth it. And we-- I arrived there on the auspices that I went out to play ping-pong. I had a ping-pong ball, a racquet, a tefillin, and a salami in there. Now, I looked like a day tripper on a bicycle going out. In case they stopped me, I said, look, I'm going to this-and-this guy. No problem. At 9 o'clock in the evening-- this was in April, end of April-- I went-- we went out to his mountainside hut, which was part of this little vineyard. And we waited till it was getting dark. We started to go through the wires, and even in the stillness of early evening night, the movement of the wire was heard by one of the Hungarian guards. But he, fortunately, had a very weak torch, and he shouted after us, stop, or I shoot. We were practically through the second set of wires. I left my overcoat, my tefillin, everything there. And I ran with this guy, who was navigate-- getting me through, for approximately a kilometer. And I will-- don't forget how out of breath I was, because it was frightening. Because, don't forget, if I was caught, I was 19 years old. I was of military age. I would have got 5 or 10 years, and I would have never seen daylight after that. Anyway, they didn't come after us, because you see what happened was the Hungarian border guards notified the Russians on the Austrian side, which was also Russian zone, and they started searching for people. It happened very often. Anyway. Danger passed. I was taken to a-- a house of-- of one of these agricultural people there. You know, the [NON-ENGLISH], as you call it. The peasant house, where I was kept till 4:00, 5:00 in the morning when there was an early bus going up to Vienna. I got some sort of identity paper. I-- at that point, I destroyed every paper of mine. I could been called Tom Jones, except I can't sing. So it-- and I could have been born wherever you like. Anyway, I was given an identity paper, which didn't look like me at all. We went into the bus. They sometime used to-- Russians-- check the buses in Wiener Neustadt on the way. And my luck, the radiator of the bus started boiling up. I spent two, three hours on the roadside with people who I didn't know and it didn't-- wouldn't have taken them long to find out that I wasn't from the locality. Came another bus from Vienna. I went up, and I arrived in the 18th bezirke in the Hernals, which the [INAUDIBLE] which was the parallel camp to the [INAUDIBLE] in Vienna for immigrants and refugees. 1949, April.
